name: BC Place
tagline: Canada's busiest World Cup venue, where the home side fights for a knockout spot under a downtown retractable roof.

why.title: Canada's Home Base
why.text: Both of Canada's decisive group games are here, vs Qatar and vs Switzerland. This is where the host nation lives in the group stage.

why.title: Busiest in Canada
why.text: Seven matches, more than any other Canadian venue, and the only one going beyond the Round of 32 with two knockout games.

why.title: The Retractable Roof
why.text: Billed as the world's largest cable-supported retractable roof, it tucks into a central pod above the videoboard and opens or closes in about 20 minutes.

why.title: Grass Grown for FIFA
why.text: Crews ripped out the artificial turf and laid a hybrid natural pitch in spring 2026, grown in the Fraser Valley and kept alive with six grow-light rigs.

gettingThere: BC Place sits right in downtown Vancouver, so most of the core is a 10-20 minute walk away. From YVR airport, ride the Canada Line SkyTrain to Waterfront, then one Expo Line stop to Stadium-Chinatown, about 30-35 minutes total for roughly CA$10.
transit: Expo Line to Stadium-Chinatown station is the closest, a 5-minute walk; exit to Beatty Street for the main gates.
Alternate route: Canada Line to Yaletown-Roundhouse, then a 10-minute walk northeast through Yaletown.
Driving and parking are strongly discouraged on match days; use a regional Park & Ride and finish by SkyTrain.
Tap a Compass Card or contactless to skip ticket lines; budget about CA$10 round-trip from the airport.
matchday: The stadium hugs the north shore of False Creek beside Yaletown, steps from Rogers Arena and the adjacent Parq Vancouver casino-resort. The official FIFA Fan Festival runs free at Hastings Park (PNE grounds) from June 11 to July 19, showing 70-plus matches on big screens.
food: Yaletown next door for craft breweries, cocktail bars, and patios within a short walk.
Gastown's cobblestone streets and lively bars, plus nearby Chinatown for late eats.
Parq Vancouver beside the stadium has a 50-foot sportsbook screen and restaurants like The Victor.
Vancouver runs strong on fresh seafood, sushi, and Asian cuisine across downtown.
tips: Get there by SkyTrain, not car; Stadium-Chinatown is a 5-minute walk and parking gridlocks after the whistle.
Beat the post-match crush by walking 10-15 minutes to Yaletown-Roundhouse, a far less mobbed station.
Pack a light jacket for evening kickoffs, which cool to 11-13C, and sunscreen for day games and the Fan Festival.
The roof handles any rain, so never let a forecast deter you from attending.
Lower bowl behind the goals carries the loudest Whitecaps Southsiders energy; west-side mid-tier seats face the videoboard and Northern Lights facade.
Clear-bag policy is in effect: bags must be clear plastic, max 12 by 6 by 12 inches.
weather: June and July in Vancouver are mild and dry with highs around 18-21C, long daylight, and a UV index up to 8; pack sunscreen and a light layer for cool evenings.
lore: Opened in 1983 for Expo 86 as the world's largest air-supported domed stadium, before a 2011 overhaul swapped the inflatable roof for the retractable one.
Hosted the 2015 FIFA Women's World Cup Final, where the USA beat Japan 5-2 on a Carli Lloyd hat-trick.
Staged the 2010 Winter Olympics opening and closing ceremonies and has hosted the CFL's Grey Cup 10 times.
Its all-time attendance record is 65,061 for an Ed Sheeran concert in September 2023; the venue's LED Northern Lights facade animates the night skyline.
